ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 229953001 refers to pasta as a substance; no specific code for boiled unsalted pasta exists; 'none' match type is used as it's not an exact or closest_parent match.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of boiled pasta is approximately 140 g (about 1 cup cooked), providing roughly 221 kcal before any sauce or topping is added.

nutrient_source.title: Boiled pasta (in unsalted water) — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for boiled pasta in unsalted water (cr_id F05070005). Saturated fat and other minor nutrients cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central NDB 20121 (Pasta, cooked, enriched, without added salt) for completeness.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 49 (low) is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white spaghetti boiled in unsalted water (GI ~49, reference glucose = 100), which is the closest approved analogous food to generic boiled white pasta in unsalted water. Glycemic load per 140 g serving = (49 × 30.86 × 1.40) / 100 ≈ 21.2. Al dente cooking reduces GI; fully cooked pasta may reach GI 55–65 (medium range). Value of 49 represents a conservative low-GI estimate for standard boiling.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1 mg per 100 g — negligible sodium content when boiled in unsalted water
hypertension.guidance: Boiled pasta cooked in unsalted water is safe for hypertension management due to its negligible sodium content (1 mg/100 g); however, patients should avoid high-sodium pasta sauces and choose wholemeal varieties to improve potassium and fibre intake for stronger DASH-diet alignment.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 30.86 g/100 g with low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100 g); GI approximately 49 (low) but glycemic load per serving is moderate (~21)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size of white boiled pasta to 100–140 g per meal, cook al dente to minimise GI, and prefer wholemeal or legume-based pasta to increase fibre and reduce post-prandial glucose excursion per ADA dietary guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.18 g/100 g), negligible sodium (1 mg/100 g), and zero cholesterol; refined carbohydrate with low fibre is a minor concern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled pasta in unsalted water is low in saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium, making it broadly compatible with a heart-healthy diet; patients should pair it with vegetable-rich, low-saturated-fat sauces and consider wholemeal pasta to improve dietary fibre intake in line with AHA cardiovascular dietary recommendations.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate with low fibre (1.8 g/100 g) and no meaningful anti-inflammatory components
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Regular white pasta provides no positive anti-inflammatory nutrients (no omega-3, antioxidants, or polyphenols); patients with rheumatoid arthritis should switch to wholemeal or legume-based pasta and pair with anti-inflammatory toppings such as extra-virgin olive oil or oily fish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled pasta in unsalted water is a plain, cooked wheat-based staple food made from durum wheat semolina pasta shapes (such as spaghetti, penne, or fusilli) boiled in unsalted water until tender. The result is pale yellow in colour with a tender, slightly chewy texture and a mild, neutral wheat flavour. Per 100 g, it provides 158 kcal, 5.8 g protein, 0.93 g fat (of which 0.18 g is saturated), and 30.86 g carbohydrate with 1.8 g dietary fibre and only 1 mg sodium. The glycemic index is approximately 49 (low) when cooked to a standard doneness, though al dente cooking lowers GI further. It is a versatile base food consumed across Western cuisines and widely adopted in Singapore as a working-lunch staple. Clinically, it is suitable for hypertension due to negligible sodium, but requires portion control and wholemeal substitution for Type 2 Diabetes management given its moderate glycemic load per serving (~21 GL). It is low in saturated fat and cholesterol, making it broadly compatible with cardiovascular dietary guidelines. It contains gluten and is not suitable for coeliac disease or gluten intolerance. It is vegan, vegetarian, halal, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: How many calories in boiled pasta?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05070005, confidence 0.95), which is an approved primary regional source. Saturated fat and minor micronutrients cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (NDB 20121, Pasta cooked enriched without added salt). GI sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database via peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. 2008 tables. All values are internally consistent and plausible for boiled white pasta.
notes: Saturated fat (0.18 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (0.11 g/100 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.32 g/100 g), trans fat (0.0 g/100 g), thiamine (0.02 mg), riboflavin (0.02 mg), niacin (0.4 mg), phosphorus (58 mg), magnesium (18 mg), zinc (0.51 mg), and water (62.1 g) cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central NDB 20121 (Pasta, cooked, enriched, without added salt) as HPB source does not report these fields. Vitamin C, vitamin A, vitamin B6, folate, vitamin E, and selenium set to -1 as these values are not reported in either HPB or USDA for plain boiled white pasta and are expected to be negligible or absent. Vitamin B12 and vitamin D set to 0.0 as plain pasta contains no animal-derived ingredients and no fortification is indicated in the HPB or USDA source entries. Cholesterol is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plain durum wheat pasta contains no cholesterol by composition). GI of 49 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white spaghetti boiled in unsalted water; actual GI may vary between 45 and 65 depending on pasta shape, cooking time, and degree of doneness. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g × serving_g) / 10000 = (49 × 30.86 × 1.40) / 100 ≈ 21.2 per 140 g serving. No ontology code was invented; FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are defensible matches for cooked pasta. SNOMED CT 229953001 is an approximate match (pasta as substance) as no specific boiled-pasta SNOMED code is available.
